31.42: The congregation's half of sheep: 337,500. The mirror of the warriors' half — the congregation receives the same absolute number, confirming the 50/50 split. The congregation's tax (1-in-50) on 337,500 sheep = 6,750 sheep for the Levites (v.47).
31.43: The congregation's half of cattle: 36,000. The parallel accounting continues. The congregation's half of each category exactly mirrors the warriors' half, confirming the mathematical precision of the accounting and the equity of the division.
31.44: The congregation's half of donkeys: 30,500. The Midianite pastoral wealth — represented by the large herds of sheep, cattle, and donkeys — now passes into Israelite ownership and is formally accounted and tithed. The tabernacle service benefits from both the Eleazar levy (small, from warriors) and the Levite levy (larger, from congregation).
31.45: The congregation's half of persons: 16,000. The full captive count (32,000 virgin girls divided equally) is distributed between warriors and congregation. The congregation's share (16,000) provides labor and household service for Israelite families — the integration of foreign captive women into Israelite households is regulated later in Deut 21:10–14.
31.46: The congregation's 16,000 persons (for completeness of the accounting sequence before the Levite levy is calculated).
31.47: Moses takes one in fifty from the congregation's share and gives it to the Levites who keep charge of the tabernacle. The Levite levy: one in fifty from the congregation's 337,500 sheep = 6,750 sheep; from 36,000 cattle = 720; from 30,500 donkeys = 610; from 16,000 persons = 320. The Levites receive approximately 10x more than Eleazar's share — consistent with the Levites' role as the general tabernacle workforce rather than the specific priestly office.
31.48: The military commanders (commanders of thousands and commanders of hundreds) come before Moses and report: 'Your servants have counted the soldiers under our command, and not one is missing.' The astonishing claim — no Israelite casualties in the Midian campaign — is theologically significant. The casualty-free holy war is unprecedented and unrepeated in the OT; it marks this war as uniquely YHWH's war, fought with such divine precision that no covenant soldier died.
31.49: The commanders present themselves in the context of the count — their accountability before Moses mirrors the census theology throughout Numbers: everyone is counted before YHWH. The casualty count of zero is a theological statement about divine protection in a sanctioned holy war, not a claim about military tactics.
31.50: As a result, the commanders bring a freewill contribution (qorban YHWH) from their individual war booty: gold articles, armlets, bracelets, signet rings, earrings, and pendants. These are personal adornments — jewelry items plundered from Midianite individuals, quite different from the official spoil accounting above. The voluntary offering comes from personal plunder not included in the official distribution.
31.51: Moses and Eleazar receive the gold from them — all worked articles. The enumeration of jewelry types (armlets/wristbands, bracelets, signet rings, earrings, pendants) reflects the actual repertoire of ANE personal jewelry attested in Egyptian and Canaanite hoards. The Midianite material culture included fine metalwork consistent with the trading networks of the incense route.
31.52: The total gold from the commanders: 16,750 shekels. A shekel weighs approximately 11.4 grams; 16,750 shekels ≈ 191 kg of gold. This is a substantial but not impossible quantity of gold jewelry for a large Midianite confederation — comparable to treasure-hoards described in Egyptian conquest records. The gold will be brought to the tabernacle as a memorial before YHWH.
31.53: Each soldier had taken his own plunder — implicitly not included in the official spoil accounting. The commanders' voluntary contribution comes out of this personal plunder, not the officially allocated shares. The distinction between official spoil (divided per command) and personal plunder (each soldier's individual acquisition) is consistent with ANE military practice and appears in the Hammurabi Code's treatment of war booty.
31.54: Moses and Eleazar bring the gold into the tent of meeting as a memorial (zikkaron) for the Israelites before YHWH. The zikkaron (memorial/reminder) is the purpose of the offering: it will stand before YHWH as a perpetual record of the campaign's divine success and the commanders' gratitude. The same word is used for the memorial stones at the Jordan crossing (Josh 4:7) and the Passover (Exod 12:14). The chapter closes with the spoil entering the sanctuary — the holy war is complete and its fruits consecrated.

25.1: Shittim (ha-Shittim, the acacias; also called Abel-Shittim, Num 33:49) was Israel's final encampment east of the Jordan, in the plains of Moab. It is the same staging ground from which Joshua later dispatches the two spies (Josh 2:1) and from which Israel crosses the Jordan (Josh 3:1). The temporal marker “while Israel was dwelling” (vayeshev Yisrael) suggests settled encampment rather than transit — a period of relative stability that made the sexual-religious entanglement possible. The proximity to Moab and Midian placed Israel in range of the Baal Peor fertility cult practiced in the surrounding region.
25.2: The Moabite women's invitation was religious, not merely social: they invited Israel to “the sacrifices of their gods” (zivchei eloheihen). Sharing a sacrificial meal in the ancient Near East meant participating in the deity's covenant — eating together at the god's table was an act of worship and alliance. Paul makes exactly this point in 1 Cor 10:18-20: those who eat at pagan sacrificial meals “participate in the altar.” Bowing to the Moabite gods followed naturally from eating at their sacrificial tables — sexual access to the women was embedded in the fertility-cult worship of Baal Peor.
25.3: Vayitsamed Yisrael le-Ba'al Pe'or — Israel “yoked itself” to Baal Peor. The verb tsamed (to yoke, attach, couple) is uniquely strong: it describes the kind of joining used for draft animals under a yoke. Israel did not merely dabble in Peor worship but was bound to it. Baal Peor is the Baal of the mountain Peor (near Shittim) — a local manifestation of the Canaanite storm-and-fertility deity whose worship characteristically involved sexual rites. The Hosea prophecy invokes this incident as Israel's paradigm of covenant betrayal: “Like grapes in the wilderness I found Israel... they came to Baal Peor and consecrated themselves to shame” (Hos 9:10).
25.4: YHWH's command: “Take all the heads of the people and put them to death publicly before the LORD, in the sight of the sun” (neged hashemesh, before/facing the sun — i.e., in full daylight, publicly, not secretly). The judicial execution of the tribal leaders bears the weight of communal guilt: the leaders failed to prevent the apostasy or participated in it. The phrase “hang them before the LORD” (hoqa' otam la-YHWH) may describe public impalement or exposure (cf. 2 Sam 21:6, 9 where the same verb appears in a ritual execution context), making the punishment a public expiatory act.
25.5: Moses delegates to the judges of Israel: each judge is to execute those in his jurisdiction who had attached themselves to Baal Peor. The distributive justice — each judge responsible for his own men — is the standard Mosaic judicial structure (Exod 18:21-26). The phrase “those of his men who attached themselves” (ha-nitsmadem le-Va'al Pe'or) uses the same root as v3's “yoked” — the verb marks those who made the religious commitment to Baal. This is targeted execution of the apostate, not mass slaughter.
25.6: The dramatic juxtaposition: while Moses and the congregation were weeping at the tent of meeting entrance (presumably in penitence for the apostasy and pleading for divine mercy), an Israelite man walked in with a Midianite woman “right in front of Moses and the whole congregation.” The Hebrew liforeihem (before the face of them) emphasizes the brazenness — it was not hidden but public defiance. The phrase “led her to his brothers” (vayaqrev el-echav) or “to his inner chamber” (a possible reading of the rare word) describes the man bringing her into a private space for the sexual-religious act, in plain view of the mourning assembly.
25.7: Phinehas son of Eleazar son of Aaron is introduced by full genealogy — the Aaronic priestly line is crucial to what follows, since YHWH's response will grant a covenant to Phinehas specifically because of his priestly standing. He “rose from among the congregation” (vayaqom mitoch ha'edah) — his action is not mob violence but a deliberate individual initiative. He takes a spear (romach, a close-combat weapon) and follows the couple. His action echoes the zeal of Levi and Simeon at Shechem (Gen 34) and anticipates the same quality praised in Elijah (1 Kings 19:10, 14) and the zealot tradition in Second Temple Judaism.
25.8: “And followed the man of Israel into the inner chamber (qubbah, a vaulted tent or domed chamber) and drove it through both of them — the man and the woman through the belly (qobatah).” The word qubbah (from the same root as qobah, vault/dome) may indicate a private inner tent used for the illicit union. Phinehas's single act kills both simultaneously. The physical detail — the spear going through the belly — underscores that the act was not symbolic but lethal. The plague stops immediately: Phinehas's intervention is the expiation that arrests the divine judgment. This is the mechanism of atonement through a human act of judgment — an unusual but OT-attested pattern (cf. Moses interceding by judgment in Exod 32:27-28).
25.9: The toll: twenty-four thousand died in the plague. Paul cites this incident in 1 Cor 10:8 but gives the number as twenty-three thousand — a discrepancy noted in ancient textual traditions. The most common explanation is that Paul rounds down or that the Hebrew text gives total plague deaths while Paul gives those who died “in one day.” The second census (Num 26:14) shows Simeon's dramatic decline from 59,300 (Num 1:23) to 22,200 — a loss of 37,100 — likely reflecting Simeon's heavy casualties in the Peor plague given that Zimri (the Israelite man killed, v14) was a Simeonite leader.
25.10: YHWH now addresses Moses directly about Phinehas — not to condemn the extrajudicial killing but to validate it as covenant faithfulness. The divine-speech formula followed by YHWH's approval of Phinehas's action is the interpretive key to the chapter: the narrator does not leave the reader to evaluate Phinehas's act; YHWH evaluates it explicitly and positively. The covenant that follows is YHWH's own validation of what Phinehas did.
25.11: “Phinehas son of Eleazar son of Aaron the priest has turned my wrath away from the Israelites by being zealous for my cause (beqan'o et-qin'ati, literally, being jealous with my jealousy) in their midst.” The verbal construction qanna + qin'ah (zealous with jealousy) uses the same root YHWH uses to describe his own character: “I YHWH your God am a jealous God” (Exod 20:5, El qanna). Phinehas acted with YHWH's own jealousy for YHWH's own honor — his zeal was not personal passion but divine character expressed through a human agent. This is why YHWH counts it as atonement: Phinehas internalized the divine jealousy.
25.12: “I am giving him my berit shalom (covenant of peace).” Shalom in covenantal contexts means not merely absence of conflict but completeness, wholeness, and ongoing well-being. The paradox is intentional: Phinehas has just committed a violent act, yet YHWH grants him a covenant of shalom. The peace is between Phinehas and YHWH — a declaration of ongoing divine favor following an act that aligned with divine holiness. It also signals the end of the plague: YHWH's anger has been appeased, and shalom is restored to the community.
25.13: “It shall be for him and his descendants after him a berit kehunat olam (covenant of perpetual/everlasting priesthood), because he was zealous for his God and made atonement for the Israelites.” The Phinehas covenant extends the Aaronic priestly covenant to Phinehas's line specifically. In later Jewish history, the line of Zadok (Solomon's high priest, 1 Kings 1:35) was traced to Phinehas (1 Chron 6:4-8, 50-53; Ezra 7:1-5). The phrase “made atonement for the Israelites” (vayechapher al-bnei Yisrael) frames Phinehas's act as priestly expiation — he stopped the plague the way a priest's sin offering stops wrath: by interposing himself and his violent action between the community and divine judgment.
25.14: The identity of the Israelite man: Zimri son of Salu, “a leader of a father's house (nasi beit-av) of the Simeonites.” He was not an ordinary Israelite but a clan chief — someone whose brazen public act carried the authority of his position, making the defiance far more dangerous. The genealogical notation here (unique to the execution narratives) serves a judicial function: the record identifies the guilty party by full name and lineage for the permanent communal memory. Zimri's Simeonite identity connects to the dramatic decline in Simeon's census numbers (ch 26).
25.15: Cozbi daughter of Zur — her name (from kazav, to lie/deceive) may be a symbolic name or editorial characterization. Zur is identified as “head of a people of a father's house in Midian” — she is not a common woman but the daughter of a Midianite tribal leader, suggesting the Baal Peor episode had an official, politically arranged character (cf. Num 31:16, where Balaam is said to have counseled this very strategy). The revelation that the Midianites orchestrated the seduction — rather than it being an organic cultural encounter — is developed in Num 31.
25.16: YHWH commands hostility toward Midian. The Moabite women initiated the encounters (v1), but YHWH's judgment focuses on the Midianites — because Midian engineered the apostasy as a weapon (cf. Num 31:16). Moab is excluded from the war mandate (cf. Deut 2:9, where YHWH forbids attacking Moab), but Midian receives it. This distinction tracks with the rest of the Pentateuch's treatment: Moab produces Ruth; Midian is destroyed in the war of Num 31.
25.17: “Treat the Midianites as enemies (tsaroru et-ha-Midyanim, be hostile to the Midianites) and strike them down.” Tsarar (to be an enemy, to press/besiege) is the same root used for Israel's covenant enemies throughout the Pentateuch. The mandate for war against Midian will be executed in ch 31, Moses's last military act before his death. The verb “strike down” (hikkitem, plural imperative) suggests comprehensive military action, not merely diplomatic hostility.
25.18: “For they dealt treacherously with you through their schemes in the Peor affair and in the matter of Cozbi, daughter of the Midianite chief, their sister, who was killed on the day of the plague in the matter of Peor.” The word nechilim (schemes, tricks, nochel) indicates deliberate deception rather than organic temptation — the Midianites used the Baal Peor seduction as a calculated weapon against Israel. Num 31:16 confirms: Balaam counseled Balak and the Midianites to use the women to cause Israel to sin. The Peor incident was the military strategy Balaam devised after failing to curse Israel directly (chs. 22-24).

26.1: “After the plague” — the second census begins in the aftermath of the Baal Peor disaster, which killed 24,000 and effectively ended the first generation. The commissioning of the census to “Moses and Eleazar son of Aaron the priest” is significant: Aaron died in Num 20:28; Eleazar has now assumed the high-priestly role, and the second census is the first major act under the new priestly leadership. The pairing of Moses (dying first generation) with Eleazar (continuing into Canaan) mirrors the transition the census itself records.
26.2: The census criteria are identical to ch 1: “all males twenty years old and upward, by their ancestral houses, all in Israel who are able to go to war.” The military-service framing (kol yotsei tzava, all who go out to war) confirms the census is a fighting roster for the conquest ahead, not merely a population count. But the census is simultaneously an inheritance register: v52-56 will use these numbers to allocate land proportionally. The second census thus serves two purposes — military readiness and covenant land-distribution.
26.3: The location — “plains of Moab beside the Jordan across from Jericho” — places this census at the threshold of the promised land. The first census (ch 1) was taken at Sinai, the mountain of covenant-formation; the second census is taken at the Jordan's edge, the moment before entry. The progression from Sinai to Moab marks the entire wilderness generation's journey in a single geographical move: from law-giving to land-entry.
26.4: “Register everyone twenty years and older, as the LORD had commanded Moses — these being the Israelites who came out of Egypt.” The phrase “who came out of Egypt” identifies the second generation's descent from the Exodus people, not their personal experience of the Exodus. The second generation was born in the wilderness (or came out as children too young for the census); they are the Israelites by lineage and covenant, heirs of the promise even though most did not personally remember Egypt. Their identity is rooted in the founding event, not in personal memory.
26.5: Reuben, the firstborn of Israel — the census begins with the firstborn tribe, maintaining the precedence of Reuben's birth order. The clan structure (mishpachot, families/clans) below the tribal level records each clan descended from Reuben's four sons. Reuben's count has declined from 46,500 (ch 1) to 43,730 — a loss of 2,770. The tribal genealogies embedded in the census provide the ancestral legitimacy for each clan's portion of the land.
26.6: Hezron and Carmi are two of Reuben's four sons (Gen 46:9). The Carmites and Hezronites are the continuing clan-lines of Reuben. The census format — from X, the Xites — is a standard genealogical-administrative listing that echoes the clan-registration (le-mishpechotam le-beit avotam) pattern used throughout Numbers.
26.7: Reuben total: 43,730. Reuben's decline (from 46,500 in Num 1) is modest compared to the catastrophic decline of Simeon (below). Reuben's losses in the wilderness period may include the Dathan and Abiram rebellion (Num 16), which was a Reubenite leadership crisis. Despite Reuben's primogeniture, the tribe is numerically smaller than Judah, Issachar, and Zebulun by this count — a trajectory that will see Reuben become a minor tribe in the settlement period.
26.8: A genealogical aside: the son of Pallu was Eliab. This single-verse note sets up the parenthetical about Dathan and Abiram in vv9-11. The census list is not merely administrative but annotated with theological commentary — the names of the faithless are embedded in the roll of the second generation as a permanent warning.
26.9: “The sons of Eliab: Nemuel, Dathan, and Abiram. This is the Dathan and Abiram who were prominent in the congregation — those who contended against Moses and Aaron in the company of Korah, when they contended against the LORD.” The interjection into the census list identifies the notorious rebels of Num 16 by name within the tribal genealogy. The insertion serves a memorial function: the second generation reading the census will recognize in their own tribal lists the names of the faithless first generation. The genealogy becomes a theological history embedded in bureaucratic form.
26.10: “The earth opened its mouth and swallowed them along with Korah when that company died — when fire devoured the two hundred fifty men, and they became a warning sign (nes, sign/banner).” Nes can mean both a warning sign and a standard/banner, suggesting the 250 men who died by fire became a permanent cautionary symbol — not just a historical event but a standing monument of the consequences of challenging YHWH's appointed order. The event is recalled within the genealogical list as a way of reading the silence: Korah's direct sons died here; only his collateral descendants continue.
26.11: “But the sons of Korah did not die.” This three-word sentence (uvnei-Qorach lo metu) is one of the most theologically significant interjections in the census. The Korahite clan survived the judgment and continues into the second generation. The Psalms contain a significant corpus attributed to “the Sons of Korah” (Pss 42, 44-49, 84-85, 87-88) — some of Israel's most profound hymns of trust and longing. The survival of Korah's sons illustrates that the sins of the fathers do not inevitably destroy the children; the covenant extends through repentance and survival.
26.12: The clans of Simeon are listed next. Their total (v14) will be 22,200 — a catastrophic decline from 59,300 in Num 1. This is a reduction of 37,100, or 63% of Simeon's first-generation number. No explicit explanation is given in ch 26, but the Zimri incident (Num 25:14) identified a Simeonite clan leader as the man killed at Peor, and the 24,000 who died in the plague were disproportionately Simeonite. The genealogical clan names here — Nemuel, Jamin, Jachin — record the survivors, not the fallen.
26.13: Zerah and Shaul complete Simeon's five clans. The clan of Shaul (son of Simeon by a Canaanite woman, Gen 46:10) is included in the census without comment — the Canaanite maternal line does not exclude Simeonites from the covenant community. The Shaulites represent the mixed-ancestry segment of the tribe that survived the wilderness generation.
26.14: Simeon total: 22,200 — the lowest of any tribe in the second census, and the most dramatic decline from the first. Simeon's territory in the later settlement (Josh 19:1-9) is embedded within Judah's allotment because Simeon was too small to hold an independent region. The tribe effectively disappears as a distinct entity in the settlement period (Deut 33 omits Simeon from Moses's tribal blessings), its remnant absorbed into Judah — a trajectory that begins with this census.
26.15: Gad's clans begin the next tribal entry. Gad was the firstborn son of Zilpah, Leah's maidservant (Gen 30:10-11). Gad's territory will be in Transjordan (Num 32), settled by agreement with Moses before the conquest. The seven clans named here (Zephon through Areli) preserve the genealogical structure of Gad's extended family at the wilderness' end.
26.16: Ozni and Eri — two of Gad's seven clans. The name Ozni (from ozen, ear) and Eri (my watcher) appear only here in the genealogical record. The Oznites and Erites are minor clans within Gad whose significance is their continuity from the generation that left Egypt (Gen 46:16) to the generation entering Canaan.
26.17: Arod and Areli complete Gad's clan list. The correspondence between the clan names here and those in Gen 46:16 (the Exodus generation) confirms the census as a genealogical register tracing continuous descent: the second generation clans are identified by their founding ancestors from the Exodus roster.
26.18: Gad total: 40,500 — down from 45,650 in ch 1, a decline of about 11%. Gad's decrease is proportional to the general wilderness attrition and consistent with a tribe that participated in the various rebellions without catastrophic singular events of judgment.
26.19: A genealogical note for Judah: “The sons of Judah: Er and Onan. But Er and Onan died in the land of Canaan.” This parenthetical references Gen 38:7-10, where YHWH killed Er and Onan for their wickedness. The census cannot enumerate clans for Er and Onan because they died without heirs; only the surviving Judahite sons (Shelah, Pharez, Zerah) generate the clans. The Canaanite-period deaths are recalled here to explain why Judah has only three active clan-lines rather than five.
26.20: Judah's three surviving clan-lines: Shelah (Judah's son by the Canaanite Shua's daughter, Gen 38:5), Pharez/Perez (Judah's son by Tamar, Gen 38:29), and Zerah (Tamar's twin, Gen 38:30). The Tamar connection — irregular and scandalous in origin — is included without shame in the Judahite genealogy. Matthew's genealogy of Jesus includes Tamar (Matt 1:3), Rahab, Ruth, and Bathsheba — women whose stories were irregular, all woven into the royal Davidic line.
26.21: The sons of Pharez (Perez) generate two further Judahite sub-clans: Hezron and Hamul (from Gen 46:12). The Hezronite line is significant: David descends from Hezron through Ram (Ruth 4:18-22; Matt 1:3-6), making the Hezronites the clan-line from which the Davidic dynasty emerges. The census records this clan at the wilderness' end; two generations later, the Hezronite Boaz will marry Ruth, and four generations after that, David will be born.
26.22: Judah total: 76,500 — up from 74,600 in ch 1, a growth of 1,900. Judah is the largest tribe in both censuses, consistent with the Judahite tradition of primacy that culminates in the Davidic monarchy. The tribe's growth contrasts with Simeon's collapse and reinforces the trajectory of Genesis' blessings: Jacob's blessing on Judah (“the scepter shall not depart from Judah”, Gen 49:10) is reflected in the tribe's numerical endurance and dominance.
26.23: Issachar's clans: from Tola, the Tolaites; from Pua, the Punites. These correspond to the sons of Issachar in Gen 46:13. Issachar was a son of Leah (Gen 30:18), the sixth of the Leah sons, and his tribe's settlement in the fertile Jezreel Valley made Issachar agriculturally prosperous in the settlement period. The census preserves the four-clan structure of the tribe from Exodus to Canaan.
26.24: Jashub and Shimron complete Issachar's four clans. The name Jashub (he returns) may be an alternate name for Job (cf. Gen 46:13 LXX, where Iob appears in this position). The Shimronites descend from Shimron, the fourth of Issachar's sons, a clan that will settle in the Jezreel area of lower Galilee.
26.25: Issachar total: 64,300 — up significantly from 54,400 in ch 1, a growth of 9,900. Issachar is among the faster-growing tribes in the second generation, which may reflect the tribe's distance from the major rebellion incidents (Korah, Peor) or simply demographic variation. In the later period, Issachar is known for its wisdom (1 Chron 12:32: “men of Issachar who had understanding of the times”).
26.26: Zebulun's three clans (Sered, Elon, Jahleel) match the sons of Zebulun in Gen 46:14. Zebulun was the sixth and last of Leah's sons (Gen 30:20) and will settle in the region between the Jezreel Valley and Galilee. Jacob's blessing on Zebulun (“by the shore of the sea he shall dwell”, Gen 49:13) and the Deuteronomy blessing (“rejoice, Zebulun, in your going out”, Deut 33:18) suggest a seafaring or trading role that matches the northern Galilean coastal proximity.
26.27: Zebulun total: 60,500 — up from 57,400 in ch 1, a modest growth of 3,100. Zebulun's continued health contrasts with the dramatic losses of the southern tribes. Matthew's Gospel notes that the region of Zebulun was the heartland of Jesus's early Galilean ministry (Matt 4:13-16), fulfilling Isaiah's prophecy about “the land of Zebulun and the land of Naphtali” seeing a great light (Isa 9:1-2).
26.28: The sons of Joseph (through his two sons Manasseh and Ephraim) are counted as two separate tribes — maintaining the twelve-tribe structure despite Levi's removal as a land-owning tribe. Jacob's adoption of Ephraim and Manasseh as his own sons (Gen 48:5) gave Joseph a double portion, consistent with the firstborn's double inheritance (Deut 21:17). The Josephite tribes will receive the central hill country of Canaan in the settlement.
26.29: Manasseh's genealogy descends through Machir (“Machir fathered Gilead”) to the sub-clan of the Gileadites. The Machir clan had already been active in Transjordanian territory (Num 32:39-40), where Machir's descendants conquered and settled Gilead before the main entry into Canaan. The Gileadite sub-tribe gave its name to the entire Transjordanian highland region east of the Jordan.
26.30: The Gileadite clans: Jeezer (= Abiezer, Josh 17:2) and Helek. Abiezer's clan will later produce Gideon (Judg 6:11, 34), one of the major judges of the settlement period. The census preserves the genealogical roots of tribes that will become major actors in the next generation's history — the census is prospective as well as retrospective.
26.31: Asriel, Shechem, and continuing Gileadite sub-clans. The clan of Shechem (not the city but a Manassite clan member) is recorded here with no reference to the Dinah incident — the clan persists as a legitimate Israelite lineage regardless of the complex history of Shechem as a place name in the tradition.
26.32: Shemida and Hepher complete the Gileadite sub-clans. The Hepherites will be significant in the next chapter: Zelophehad was “son of Hepher” (v33), and his daughters' case about female inheritance (Num 27) will generate a legal precedent for all of Israel. The census embeds in v33 the genealogical information that makes the Zelophehad case intelligible.
26.33: “Zelophehad son of Hepher had no sons, only daughters: Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah.” This sentence is the genealogical setup for Num 27:1-11, where these five daughters petition Moses for their father's inheritance. The census list is doing double duty: recording the Hepherite population and flagging the inheritance problem that requires legal resolution. The women are named in the census — a rare distinction that signals their legal significance. Their case will generate a ruling that permanently modifies Israelite inheritance law.
26.34: Manasseh total: 52,700 — up dramatically from 32,200 in ch 1, a growth of 20,500 (64%). Manasseh is the fastest-growing tribe in the second census, a striking contrast to Ephraim's decline (below). The reversal of primogeniture — Jacob blessed Ephraim over Manasseh (Gen 48:19) — appears to reverse in the census data: Manasseh outgrows Ephraim in the second generation. Both tribes' land allotments in ch 17 of Joshua will reflect their relative sizes.
26.35: Ephraim's clans: Shuthelah, Becher, and Tahan (Gen 46:20 LXX; cf. Gen 46:20 MT which has different names for Ephraim's sons — textual complexity in the Josephite genealogies). Shuthelah is the primary Ephraimite clan; the name appears also in 1 Chron 7:20-27, which traces the Ephraimite lineage down to Joshua son of Nun — confirming that Joshua descends from Ephraim's Shuthelah line.
26.36: The sub-clan of Eran, son of Shuthelah, rounds out the Ephraimite genealogy. The Eranite clan is not mentioned elsewhere in the OT; its preservation here in the census is the sole record of this Ephraimite sub-family's existence. The level of genealogical detail in the census — tracking not just tribal founders but their children and grandchildren — reflects a sophisticated administrative record-keeping tradition.
26.37: Ephraim total: 32,500 — down from 40,500 in ch 1, a decline of 8,000. Ephraim's decrease contrasts with Manasseh's dramatic growth. The combined Joseph tribes (52,700 + 32,500 = 85,200) exceed the single-tribe totals of any other tribe except Judah (76,500), confirming Jacob's prediction that Ephraim and Manasseh would be “a multitude of nations” (Gen 48:19). Despite the decline, Ephraim will emerge as the dominant northern tribe after the settlement, eventually giving its name to the Northern Kingdom.
26.38: Benjamin's clans begin the enumeration of the smaller Rachel-group tribes. Five clans are named: Bela, Ashbel, Ahiram, Shupham, and Hupham — descending from Benjamin's sons (Gen 46:21 lists ten sons, but the clan-list here traces five surviving lines). Benjamin's territory will be the smallest allotment in Canaan, positioned between Judah and Ephraim, but strategically placed at the center of the future kingdom. Jerusalem is on the Benjamin-Judah border.
26.39: The Shuphamites and Huphamites are two of Benjamin's clan-lines not individually attested outside this census. The Hupham/Huppim connection appears in 1 Chron 7:12 in a compressed form. The Benjaminite clan lists here and in 1 Chron 7-8 diverge somewhat, reflecting the tribal record's preservation across different archival traditions.
26.40: An embedded genealogical note: “the sons of Bela were Ard and Naaman,” generating two further Benjaminite sub-clans (the Ardites and the Naamites). Ard and Naaman appear as sons (not grandsons) of Benjamin in Gen 46:21, but here as grandsons — a genealogical compression common in OT tribal records. The Naaman connection becomes relevant in later tradition: Naaman the Syrian (2 Kings 5) bears the same name as this Benjaminite clan-founder, though no relationship is established.
26.41: Benjamin total: 45,600 — up from 35,400 in ch 1, a growth of 10,200. Benjamin's significant growth in the second generation is notable given the catastrophic decimation of Benjamin in the later civil war (Judg 20-21, where all but 600 Benjaminite men were killed). The relative strength of Benjamin at this point makes the later near-extinction all the more striking. Paul the apostle identifies as a Benjaminite (Phil 3:5), a tribe whose survival was itself a miraculous preservation.
26.42: Dan's clans: only one is named — Shuham (also spelled Hushim in Gen 46:23), generating the Shuhamites. Dan has only a single clan-line, the smallest clan-structure of any tribe. Dan's large census total despite a single genealogical line suggests that Shuham's descendants multiplied dramatically. The single-clan structure may also reflect the absorption of neighboring peoples or the loss of other Danite lines in the wilderness period.
26.43: Dan total: 64,400 — up from 62,700 in ch 1, a slight increase of 1,700. Dan maintains its position as one of the larger tribes, second only to Judah in this census. The Danite tendency to migrate (they eventually settle far north at Laish/Dan, Judg 18) may account for population stability through geographic flexibility rather than rootedness in a single region.
26.44: Asher's clans begin with Jimna, Jesui, and Beriah — three of the four sons named in Gen 46:17. Asher was the eighth son of Jacob, born to Zilpah (Gen 30:12-13). His name (happy/blessed) connects to Leah's exclamation at his birth. Asher's territory in the settlement will be the northwestern coastal strip from Carmel to Phoenicia — prosperous and strategically placed.
26.45: The Heberites and Malchielites descend from Beriah's two sons. These sub-clans within Asher are attested in 1 Chron 7:31-32. The level of sub-clan detail (tribe → clan → sub-clan) in the census reflects a three-tier genealogical system used for land allocation: the portion within the tribe's region was further divided by clan and sub-clan.
26.46: “The name of Asher's daughter was Serah.” This single sentence — the only woman named in the tribal census sections — is remarkable. Serah daughter of Asher appears in the Exodus generation census (Gen 46:17) and here in the second generation census. Later Jewish tradition (Midrash Tanchuma) credits Serah with great longevity and wisdom, making her a living link between the patriarchs and the Exodus generation. Her inclusion in both censuses (across a forty-year gap) may indicate she was still alive — one of the few first-generation survivors.
26.47: Asher total: 53,400 — up from 41,500 in ch 1, a growth of 11,900. Asher is among the fastest-growing tribes in the second census. Moses's blessing of Asher (Deut 33:24-25: “Asher is most blessed of sons; let him be the favorite of his brothers”) corresponds to the tribe's numerical strength and prosperity at this point. The growth trajectory confirms Asher as one of the healthier tribes entering Canaan.
26.48: Naphtali's clans: Jahzeel, Guni, Jezer, and Shillem — the four sons of Naphtali (Gen 46:24). Naphtali was the sixth son of Jacob, born to Bilhah, Rachel's maidservant (Gen 30:8). His name (my wrestling) reflects Rachel's sense of competition with Leah. Naphtali's settlement in upper Galilee will place the tribe in the region Isaiah designates for the coming light (Isa 9:1-2), the area of Jesus's Galilean ministry (Matt 4:15-16).
26.49: Jezer and Shillem, the third and fourth Naphtalite clans. All four of Naphtali's sons have surviving clan-lines at this point, suggesting the tribe maintained a stable four-clan structure through the wilderness. The Naphtalite leader Ahira son of Enan appeared in the census (Num 1:15), the tribal offerings (Num 7:78), and the march order (Num 10:27) — a consistent administrative figure across the Numbers records.
26.50: Naphtali total: 45,400 — down from 53,400 in ch 1, a decline of 8,000. Naphtali is one of the declining tribes in the second census, a reversal from its strong first-generation showing. The reason for Naphtali's decline is not recorded. In the settlement period, Naphtali's territory will be contested by Canaanites and Phoenicians, consistent with a tribe that may have had more border exposure and conflict even in the wilderness period.
26.51: The grand total of the second census: 601,730 — down from 603,550 in ch 1, a decline of 1,820. Despite forty years in the wilderness, an entire generation's death, two major plague-and-rebellion events (Num 16 and Num 25), and innumerable hardships, Israel's military-age male population is virtually unchanged. The near-stability of the total is the census's quiet theological statement: YHWH's covenant population endures. The one generation that died was replaced by another that is ready for the conquest.
26.52: A new divine speech opens the land-allocation section (vv52-56). The transition from census-as-roster to census-as-inheritance-register is made explicit: “to these the land shall be divided as an inheritance” (leeileh techaleq ha-aretz leNachalah). The census numbers are not merely administrative data but the basis for covenant-land distribution. Every man counted receives a proportional share; the counting is simultaneously the measuring of the promise.
26.53: “To these the land shall be divided as an inheritance according to the number of names.” Berov shemot (by the multitude of names) — the number of registered individuals determines the size of each tribe's portion. The formula is fair: more people → more land; fewer people → less land. The land is distributed not by tribal history, military power, or seniority of birth but by current registered population. This principle of proportional inheritance is democratic in its application of the covenant promise.
26.54: “To a larger group give a larger inheritance, and to a smaller group a smaller one. Each shall receive its inheritance proportional to those numbered in it.” The principle is explicit and repeated for emphasis. The fairness of the allocation system is YHWH's design — no tribe can claim more than its registered size warrants. This prevents the larger tribes from dominating the covenant allocation and the smaller tribes from being marginalized. The proportional distribution is a covenant equity principle.
26.55: “Nevertheless, the land shall be divided by lot (begoral techalek et-ha-aretz).” Despite the proportional principle of vv53-54, the specific location of each tribe's portion within Canaan is determined by lot (goral). The lot is divine: Prov 16:33 states “the lot is cast into the lap, but its every decision is from YHWH.” The combination of human calculation (proportional size) and divine determination (lot for location) means that each tribe's where is YHWH's gift, while the how much is proportional to covenant-participation. No tribe gets to claim Canaan's best land by military power or political clout.
26.56: “By lot the possession shall be divided, whether between a larger or smaller group.” The restatement of the lot principle affirms that it applies to all tribes equally — the lot respects no tribal precedence. Larger tribes receive more land in area, but the quality and location of the land is not manipulated to favor prominent tribes. The lot system is the covenant's mechanism for resolving the inevitable tension between different tribes' preferences and the practical geography of Canaan.
26.57: The Levite census follows separately (vv57-62) because the Levites receive no land inheritance — YHWH is their inheritance (Num 18:20). The Levite clan-list: Gershon, Kohath, Merari — the same three clans as the Sinai and wilderness censuses (Num 3-4). A fourth group is added: “from Gershon, the Libnites and the Hebronites and the Mahlites and the Mushites and the Korathites.”
26.58: The Levite sub-clans are listed more fully here: Libnite, Hebronite, Mahlite, Mushite, and Korathite. Kohath fathered Amram — the critical genealogical link to Moses, Aaron, and Miriam. The Korahites (from Izhar son of Kohath) were the clan of Korah the rebel (ch 16) whose sons survived (v11). Their inclusion in the clan list without erasure shows that genealogical record was not retroactively altered for theological reasons — the embarrassing history is preserved intact.
26.59: “The name of Amram's wife was Jochebed, daughter of Levi, who was born to Levi in Egypt.” Jochebed is the mother of Aaron, Moses, and Miriam — the three founding leaders of the Exodus generation. Her identification as “daughter of Levi” establishes her as a first-generation Levite (born in Egypt), making Moses a second-generation Levite by direct descent on both sides (Amram was also a Kohathite Levite). The matrilineal notation is unusual in OT genealogies and reflects Jochebed's historical importance as the mother of Israel's prophetic and priestly founders.
26.60: Aaron's four sons: Nadab, Abihu, Eleazar, and Ithamar — the same four who were ordained at Sinai (Lev 8-9). The census records them as the Aaronic priestly family, though the continuation immediately notes the deaths of the two elder sons. Eleazar and Ithamar are the surviving lines through whom the Aaronic priesthood continues.
26.61: “Nadab and Abihu died when they offered unauthorized fire before the LORD.” The reference is to Lev 10:1-2, where Nadab and Abihu were consumed by divine fire for offering “strange/unauthorized fire” (esh zarah). Their deaths in the census list serve the same function as the Dathan-Abiram note in v9: the genealogical record embeds the theological warning. The priesthood's holiness-requirement is not merely a legal abstraction but a matter of life and death, as the sons of Aaron themselves discovered.
26.62: The Levite total: 23,000 males from a month old and upward — compared to 22,000 in Num 3:39, a growth of 1,000. The age criterion for Levites is different: starting from a month old (not twenty years), reflecting the fact that Levites are counted as a substitute for firstborns (Num 3:40-51), not as a military force. The Levites “were not enrolled among the Israelites, because there was no inheritance given to them among the Israelites.” Their separate count underlines their separate status: they are YHWH's portion, not Israel's military resource.
26.63: “These are those numbered by Moses and Eleazar the priest, who enrolled the Israelites in the plains of Moab beside the Jordan across from Jericho.” The location notation closes the census with a geographical marker: the Jordan and Jericho are visible from here. The second census was conducted at the threshold of the promise — the registered community is the one that will cross the river and receive the land.
26.64: “But not one of these was among those Moses and Aaron had registered when they counted the Israelites in the wilderness of Sinai.” The critical statement: there is zero overlap between the first census (ch 1) and the second census (ch 26), except for the two exceptions in v65. The entire registered military force of Israel has been replaced in forty years. This is the census's ultimate theological statement about Numbers' central narrative: YHWH said the first generation would die in the wilderness (Num 14:28-35), and it did. The second census is the proof of that prophecy's complete fulfillment.
26.65: “For the LORD had said of them: They shall surely die in the wilderness. And not one of them was left except Caleb son of Jephunneh and Joshua son of Nun.” The census ends with the two exceptions to the death-decree: Caleb and Joshua — the only two of the twelve spies who brought a faithful report (Num 14:6-9, 30). Their survival is not accidental survival of strong men; it is the fulfillment of YHWH's explicit promise in Num 14:30: “not one shall enter the land, except Caleb son of Jephunneh and Joshua son of Nun.” The census's final verse is Numbers' theological summary: YHWH's word of judgment was precise, complete, and exactly fulfilled; his word of mercy to the faithful was equally precise and equally fulfilled.

27.1: The daughters of Zelophehad appear at a critical moment: the census of ch26 has just assigned land by family heads with male names, and no provision exists for a man who dies with daughters but no sons. The five daughters — Mahlah, Noah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Tirzah — are named as individuals, unusually specific in a text that often refers to women collectively or anonymously. Their full genealogy (Zelophehad → Hepher → Gilead → Machir → Manasseh) establishes their legitimate lineage within a major tribe, giving their claim maximum legal standing.
27.2: They stand before (wayyaʿămōḏnâ lip̄nê) Moses, Eleazar, the leaders, and the whole congregation — the full public judicial assembly at the sanctuary entrance. Ancient legal proceedings required public witnesses; presenting the claim before the entire assembled community transforms a family dispute into constitutional law. The location — the tent of meeting's entrance — places the claim in the divine court, not merely before human judges.
27.3: The daughters establish two facts: (1) their father died from his own sin, not from Korah's rebellion (bəʿăḏat Qōraḥ lōʾ-hāyâ); and (2) he left no sons. The first point matters legally: Korah's rebels and their households were destroyed (yibbālēʿ, 16:33), which may have forfeited their inheritance rights entirely. Zelophehad's ordinary death in the wilderness keeps his lineage's land claim intact; only the succession mechanism is missing.
27.4: The daughters' argument: lāmmâ yiggāraʿ šem-ʾāḇînû mittôk mišpaḥtô — why should our father's name be diminished from his clan? The inheritance of land is the perpetuation of family identity; land without an heir means the family name is erased from tribal memory. The appeal is not purely economic but genealogical-covenantal: every family was to have its place in the tribal inheritance of the promised land, and that place should not disappear due to an accident of birth.
27.5: wayyaqrēḇ Moshe ʾet-mišpāṭān lip̄nê YHWH — Moses brought their case before YHWH. The verb qāraḇ (draw near) is used for priestly approach; Moses here acts as the supreme legal mediator. He does not decide the case himself but refers it to divine judgment. This referral pattern — used also in the Sabbath-breaker case (Num 15:34) and the Passover impurity case (Num 9:8) — shows the development of Mosaic case law as responsive divine revelation rather than systematic pre-established code.
27.6: YHWH's direct response through Moses establishes the divine validation of the daughters' claim. The legislative response is in two parts: an immediate ruling for this specific case (v7) and a general inheritance law for future cases (vv8-11). The specific case generates the general principle — characteristic of the casuistic (case-law) legislative style of P material throughout Numbers.
27.7: kēn ḇənôt Ṣəlop̄ḥāḏ dōḇərôt — the daughters of Zelophehad speak rightly. The divine affirmation is unequivocal. YHWH does not merely permit their request; he declares them to be right. The inheritance shall pass to them, and their father's possession shall transfer through them. The ruling is immediately implemented: give them a hereditary possession among their father's brothers.
27.8: The general rule: if a man dies without a son, his inheritance passes to his daughter. The rule creates a hierarchy: son → daughter → brothers → father's brothers → nearest clan relative. This hierarchy expands the inheritance options well beyond the default male-primogeniture norm, while maintaining patrilineal structure. ANE parallel: Code of Hammurabi §§171-174 allowed daughters to inherit as malkatum in certain circumstances; Middle Assyrian Laws §§29-33 addressed daughter-inheritance from childless fathers. Israel's ruling goes further in explicitly validating daughters' claims as equal to sons' in the absence of male heirs.
27.9: If no daughter exists, inheritance passes to the brothers. The sequence ensures that land remains within the closest possible kinship circle before passing to more distant relatives — land was not simply property but the material form of covenant inheritance, the family's portion in the promised land. The loss of land to unrelated parties would represent a covenant failure.
27.10: If no brothers, to the father's brothers (uncles). The kinship succession model mirrors the gōʾēl (kinsman-redeemer) pattern of Leviticus 25 and Ruth, where the closest available male relative had both the right and obligation to redeem family property. The inheritance succession law and the redemption institution are complementary structures for keeping land within its covenant-assigned family lines.
27.11: If no father's brothers, to the nearest relative in the family. The phrase lišʾērô haqqārōḇ ʾēlāyw — to the one nearest to him from his family — allows for the extension of inheritance to any identifiable family connection rather than letting the land revert to tribal common land. This is declared a ḥuqqat mišpāṭ — a statute of judgment — permanent for all Israel. The case of Zelophehad's daughters thus became constitutional inheritance law, cited later in Josh 17:3-6 when they claimed their allotment.
27.12: YHWH commands Moses to ascend the Abarim mountains — the range culminating in Nebo/Pisgah, from whose height Canaan is visible. The viewing of the land before death is structured as a gift within the judgment: Moses cannot enter, but he may see. The land remains as real and promised as ever; Moses's personal exclusion does not diminish the promise's certainty for the generation that will cross.
27.13: Moses will be gathered to his people (wəneʾesap̄tā ʾel-ʿammekā) as Aaron was gathered to his (20:24-29). The parallel construction frames Moses's death as a completion parallel to Aaron's — the two founding leaders of the exodus generation will die in the same wilderness, by the same divine appointment, before the same land they served toward. The phrase marks death as a covenant homecoming rather than mere biological ending.
27.14: The reason for Moses's exclusion is stated again: rebellion against YHWH's command at the waters of Meribah-Kadesh. The specific charge — mərîtem ʾet-pî — you rebelled against my mouth — uses the word for speaking against divine command. The phrase ləhaqdîšênî — to sanctify/display my holiness — restates Num 20:12's verdict: Moses's failure was not merely disobedience but a failure to communicate YHWH's character accurately before the congregation.
27.15: Moses's response to his own death sentence is to pray for a successor — not to appeal the verdict, not to grieve, but to intercede for the community he has led. This self-forgetting intercession after personal judgment defines Moses's character: the man who cannot enter the land still cares more about who will lead the people into it than about his own fate.
27.16: yip̄qōḏ YHWH ʾĕlōhê hārûḥōt ləkol-bāśār ʾîš ʿal-hāʿēḏâ — may YHWH, the God of the spirits of all flesh, appoint a man over the congregation. The address of God as ʾĕlōhê hārûḥōt ləkol-bāśār — the God who breathed life into all flesh — invokes the creator's authority over life and its Spirit, grounding Moses's request in the deepest possible theological claim: the one who gives the spirit of life to all humans can choose who carries his spirit for leading.
27.17: The qualifications for the successor: ʾăšer-yēṣēʾ lip̄nêhem wǎʾăšer yāḇōʾ lip̄nêhem — one who will go out before them and come in before them. The military leadership idiom (yāṣāʾ... bāʾ before the people) describes a commander who personally leads in battle rather than directing from the rear. The pastoral dimension follows: wƏʾăšer yôṣîʾēm wǎʾăšer yəḇîʾēm — who will lead them out and bring them in. Moses's concern is not succession of position but succession of shepherding character. The phrase ʾăšer lōʾ-tihyeh ʿăḏat YHWH kaṣṣōʾn ʾăšer ʾên-lāhem rōʿeh — so that YHWH's congregation will not be like sheep without a shepherd — became the canonical shepherd-without-leader image used by Ezekiel (34:5), Zechariah (10:2), and Jesus (Matt 9:36; Mark 6:34).
27.18: YHWH designates Joshua son of Nun — previously Moses's attendant (Exod 24:13; 33:11; Num 11:28) and one of the two faithful spies (Num 14:6-9,30,38) — as the successor. The qualification: ʾîš ʾăšer-rûaḥ bô — a man in whom the Spirit resides. This Sprit-qualification echoes the description of the 70 elders (Num 11:25-29) and anticipates the prophetic-Spirit theme in Joshua and the Judges. The laying on of hands (səmiḵâ) is the transfer gesture; in rabbinic Judaism this became the standard ordination ceremony (Sanhedrin 13b).
27.19: The commissioning must happen before Eleazar the priest and the whole congregation — public, witnessed, communal. The transparency of the succession prevents later disputes: everyone who will follow Joshua has witnessed his divine appointment. This public commissioning pattern becomes the template for subsequent leadership transitions: Moses commissions Joshua publicly (Deut 31:7-8,23), Samuel anoints David publicly (1 Sam 16:13), and the apostolic laying-on-of-hands in Acts follows the same communal principle.
27.20: wənātattā mêhôḏəkā ʿālāyw — transfer some of your authority/majesty to him. The word הוֹד (hôḏ) — splendor, majesty, authority — describes the divine radiance that characterizes legitimate authority. Moses does not transfer all his authority (v21: Joshua will consult the Urim, while Moses spoke face to face) but enough for the congregation to obey. The partial transfer creates a hierarchy: Joshua is legitimate successor but not Moses's functional equal.
27.21: Joshua's consultative mode: he shall stand before Eleazar the priest, who will inquire for him by the אוּרִים (ûrîm) — the oracular objects kept by the high priest (Exod 28:30; Lev 8:8). The Urim and Thummim provided binary yes/no decisions in judicial and military matters; the high priest mediated the divine answer. Moses had direct divine access (YHWH spoke with him mouth-to-mouth, Num 12:8); Joshua operates through priestly mediation. This structural distinction marks the post-Mosaic period as one of more mediated revelation — a pattern that will characterize the entire Judges-Kings-Prophets era.
27.22: Moses executes the commissioning with full compliance: takes Joshua, presents him before Eleazar and the congregation. The compliance formula (kaʾăšer ṣiwwâ YHWH ʾet-Moshe) marks this as another act of covenantal obedience. In the act of commissioning his own successor, Moses models the humility of covenant leadership: the leader serves the community's future, not his own position's perpetuation.
27.23: The laying on of hands and commissioning complete the succession ceremony. Numbers ends in ch36; this appointment in ch27 functions as the theological turning point: from here, the narrative moves toward Moses's death and Joshua's entry. The ch27 Zelophehad ruling and Joshua commissioning are paired as law and leadership — the two things Israel needs most as it enters the land: just property law and capable, spirit-filled leadership.

28.1: The festival offering calendar of chs 28-29 begins a new legislative unit addressing the moadîm — appointed times. These chapters supplement the calendar of Lev 23 with precise offering quantities that Leviticus does not specify. The placement of the calendar legislation here — after the census and before the Transjordanian campaigns — may reflect a preparation for settled land-worship after the conquest, or may be the narrative completion of the Sinai legislation that began in Leviticus.
28.2: ʾet-qorḇānî laḥmî ləʾiššay rêaḥ nîḥōḥî — my offering, the food of my fire offerings, a pleasing aroma to me. The language frames the sacrificial system as YHWH's provision for his own communal meals with Israel — an ANE idiom where deity-worship involved the feeding of the god. The P tradition uses this language while simultaneously affirming that YHWH does not literally eat (Ps 50:12-13); the fire offering as pleasing aroma is a relational/acceptance metaphor, not a literal feeding. The command is stated as personal divine preference: observe at its appointed time.
28.3: The תָּמִיד (tāmîḏ) — the regular/perpetual offering — two yearling male lambs every day. The tamid became the institutional heartbeat of both Tabernacle and Temple; the Mishnah tractate Tamid describes the Second Temple's daily offering in minute detail across 7 chapters. In the post-Temple era, the morning and afternoon prayer services (Shacharit and Minchah) were understood to replace the tamid as the daily acts of covenant maintenance.
28.4: One lamb in the morning (babbōqer) and one at twilight (bên hāʿarbayyim — between the two evenings). The two-part daily offering structurally sanctifies the entire day: morning and evening frames enclose all human activity within the covenant acknowledgment. The Passover lamb was slaughtered at the same twilight time (Exod 12:6), connecting the daily maintenance offering to the founding redemptive sacrifice.
28.5: The grain accompaniment: one-tenth of an ephah of fine flour mixed with oil — matching the specifications of Num 15:4 for the lamb-sized grain offering. The consistency with ch15's graduated offering system integrates the tamid into the broader sacrificial framework. The tamid represents the minimum standard of daily covenant maintenance; all other offerings are additions to this baseline.
28.6: The tamid is characterized as ʿōlat tāmîḏ — the regular/perpetual burnt offering — established at Sinai (hāʿăśûyâ bəhar Sînay). The Sinai institution establishes the tamid as a permanent covenant obligation, not a later innovation. The phrase gives the daily offering its maximum authoritative grounding: it originated at the foundational covenant-moment and reflects YHWH's ongoing claim on each day of Israel's life.
28.7: The drink offering (nesek) for the tamid: one-fourth of a hin of fermented drink (šēkār). The specification of šēkār — strong drink, likely barley beer — rather than wine for the daily offering is unusual; most other offerings specify wine. This may reflect the availability of barley beer as the ordinary daily beverage in the ancient Near East versus wine as more festive; the tamid uses what is daily rather than what is special.
28.8: The evening lamb receives the same grain and drink offering as the morning lamb. The symmetry of morning and evening — identical sacrifices framing the day — creates a liturgical bracket: Israel begins and ends each day with the same act of covenant acknowledgment, making the entire day a continuous act of worship.
28.9: The Sabbath offering doubles the daily: two lambs plus a double grain offering. The Sabbath receives exactly twice the regular weekday offering, expressing the Sabbath's qualitative distinction through quantitative intensification. The doubled offering does not replace the tamid (v10) but supplements it; on the Sabbath, Israel brings both sets of offerings.
28.10: The Sabbath offering is in addition to (ʿal) the regular daily tamid and its drink offering. The additive structure of the festival calendar — each higher-level offering builds on rather than replaces the lower — creates a hierarchy of covenant intensity: daily offering + Sabbath addition + new moon addition + festival addition. The total offering burden increases with the occasion's significance.
28.11: The רֹאשׁ חֹדֶשׁ (rōʾš ḥōḏeš) — New Moon — offering: two young bulls, one ram, seven yearling male lambs. The lunar calendar governed the Israelite festival schedule; the new moon was both a calendar-reset marker and a monthly covenant celebration. New moon observance appears throughout the Hebrew Bible as a significant worship occasion (1 Sam 20:5,18,24-27; Isa 1:13-14; Amos 8:5; Hos 2:11), though not among the three pilgrimage festivals. The new moon offering is the most elaborate between the daily and the festival offerings.
28.12: The graduated grain offering quantities for the new moon follow the bull-ram-lamb scale established in Num 15: three-tenths for each bull, two-tenths for the ram. The consistent scaling across all occasions (daily, Sabbath, new moon, festivals) reflects the P tradition's unified sacrificial mathematics — the same proportional system applies regardless of the occasion's scale.
28.13: One-tenth of an ephah for each of the seven lambs at the new moon. The seven lambs — the number of completeness — in the new moon offering mirrors the seven lambs in the Sukkot offering (29:13ff) and in the Passover offering (v19), suggesting a structural connection between the monthly renewal and the major annual festivals.
28.14: The drink offerings at the new moon are specified with particular precision: half a hin of wine per bull, one-third of a hin per ram, one-quarter per lamb. The increase over the daily tamid quantities (one-quarter per lamb, v7) signals the new moon's elevated status. The total new moon drink offering exceeds the daily by a substantial amount, making wine consumption at the new moon a significant community event.
28.15: One male goat (śəʿîr ʿizzîm ʾeḥāḏ) as a sin offering at the new moon — the same sin-offering goat that appears at every festival throughout chs 28-29. The monthly sin offering cleanses the accumulated unintentional errors of the past month, providing a regular mechanism for communal purification between the major annual atoning events. The new moon became a minor but regular atonement moment in Israel's ritual calendar.
28.16: Passover (14th of the first month) is noted briefly as a single-verse marker — the instructions are already in Exod 12 and Lev 23:5. Chs 28-29 focus on the offering quantities, not the ceremony; Passover's ceremony is legislated elsewhere, while Numbers specifies only the sacrificial complement. The 14th day is the day of slaughter; vv17-25 cover the seven-day Unleavened Bread festival that follows.
28.17: The Festival of Unleavened Bread (Chag HaMatzot) begins on 15th Nisan and runs seven days. The combination of Passover (14th) and Unleavened Bread (15th-21st) creates an eight-day spring festival complex that Leviticus and Deuteronomy treat as a single integrated event. The removal of leaven from Israelite homes for seven days was a complete domestic purge — all traces of the old fermentation removed, signaling both the hasty departure from Egypt and the beginning of a new season.
28.18: The first day of Unleavened Bread is a miqrāʾ-qōḏeš — a holy assembly/convocation. The term מִקְרָא קֹדֶשׁ (miqrāʾ-qōḏeš) — literally a sacred calling — designates a day on which normal labor is prohibited and the community assembles for worship. Six of Israel's annual occasions carry this status: the first and seventh days of Unleavened Bread, Shavuot, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, and Shemini Atzeret (29:35).
28.19: The Unleavened Bread festival offering: two bulls, one ram, seven lambs — identical to the Shavuot offering (vv27), the Rosh Hashanah offering (29:2), and the Yom Kippur offering (29:8). The numerical uniformity of these four major offerings may indicate a common sacrificial template for major holy assemblies, with Sukkot's escalating bulls (29:12-38) representing a separate, more elaborate pattern.
28.20: The grain offerings follow the standard graduated scale: three-tenths per bull, two-tenths per ram. The festival grain offerings are substantially larger than the daily tamid's one-tenth, reflecting the festival's greater intensity of covenant acknowledgment. The precision of quantities across all festivals ensures that the calendar's sacrificial requirements are unambiguous and consistent.
28.21: One-tenth per lamb for the seven lambs of the Unleavened Bread offering. The seven lambs appearing consistently across major festivals (Unleavened Bread, Shavuot, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, Sukkot) create a numerical signature for covenant-celebration occasions — the number of completeness applied to the flock-offering as the standard festive sacrificial unit.
28.22: One male goat as sin offering during Unleavened Bread — the same sin-offering structure as the new moon and all major festivals. The goat sin offering provides the atoning dimension for each festival occasion: celebration of covenant blessing is paired with acknowledgment of covenant failure, ensuring that festival worship does not bypass the community's ongoing need for atonement.
28.23: All festival offerings are in addition to (milelāḏ ʿōlat habbōqer) the regular morning tamid. The additive structure means that on high-festival days the total offering burden was substantial — the daily tamid + Sabbath addition (if applicable) + festival offering all accumulate. This layering prevents festivals from replacing daily worship; they intensify the covenant relationship while the tamid maintains its continuous baseline.
28.24: The same pattern of food-offering repeats each of the seven days of Unleavened Bread. The seven-day repetition builds the festival's duration into the sacrificial calendar: the Exodus is not commemorated in a single day's offering but across a full week's repeated acts of covenant renewal. The repetition embodies the Unleavened Bread's theological point: the separation from Egypt's leaven is not a moment but a sustained orientation.
28.25: The seventh day of Unleavened Bread is also a holy assembly with no regular work — mirroring the first day (v18). The festival's boundaries are marked by matched holy assemblies, giving the entire week the character of a sacred bracket: enter with holiness, travel through seven days of offering, exit with holiness. The seventh-day holy assembly also recalls the Sabbath structure: seven days leading to a sacred culmination.
28.26: The Festival of Firstfruits — Yôm habbikkûrîm, identified here as the Day of Firstfruits — is what Exodus 23:16 calls the Festival of Weeks (Shavuot) and Deut 16:9-12 connects to counting seven weeks from the first harvest. The name bikkûrîm — firstfruits — frames Shavuot primarily as an agricultural thanksgiving: the first grain of the new harvest is presented to YHWH. The Septuagint calls it Pentecost (fiftieth day), reflecting the counting tradition of Lev 23:15-16.
28.27: The Shavuot offering: two bulls, one ram, seven lambs — identical to the Passover festival offering. The structural parallel between Passover and Shavuot in the Numbers calendar connects the spring grain festival to the spring exodus festival as components of a single covenant season. In Second Temple and early Christian interpretation, Shavuot at Sinai (when Torah was given) was linked to Shavuot in Acts 2 (when the Spirit was given) as fulfillment-of-type.
28.28: The grain offerings for Shavuot follow the same graduated scale as all major festivals: three-tenths per bull, two-tenths for the ram. The consistency of the offering math across all major festivals creates a unified sacrificial language; each festival speaks with the same numerical vocabulary while varying in duration, season, and ceremony.
28.29: One-tenth per lamb for the seven lambs of Shavuot. The seven-lamb signature applies here as throughout the festival calendar. The precision of one-tenth per lamb (rather than a total grain offering for all seven) reflects the individual accountability built into the offering system: each animal has its specified accompaniment.
28.30: One male goat for Shavuot's sin offering — the ubiquitous festival atonement offering. The goat sin offering's presence at every major festival (new moon, Passover week, Shavuot, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur, each day of Sukkot) makes it the structural atonement companion to all festive celebration. No major occasion in Israel's calendar passes without an accompanying acknowledgment of sin and its need for divine forgiveness.
28.31: All Shavuot offerings are in addition to the regular tamid. The closing additive formula (milelāḏ ʿōlat hattāmîḏ) closes the Shavuot section as it closed the Unleavened Bread section (v23). The reiteration of additive structure at each unit's close ensures that the layered system is not misread as replacement; the tamid persists beneath all festival observance as the daily covenant foundation.

29.1: The first day of the seventh month is a yôm tərûʿâ — a day of trumpet-sounding. This is Rosh Hashanah (New Year) in the later Jewish calendar, though the term itself does not appear in Numbers 29; the first-of-the-seventh-month designation is expanded in Lev 23:24 as a šabbāṭôn zikrôn tərûʿâ (a Sabbath of remembrance with trumpet-blowing). The seventh month is the most sacrifice-laden month of the calendar: Rosh Hashanah (1st), Yom Kippur (10th), and Sukkot (15th-22nd) are all contained within it.
29.2: The Rosh Hashanah offering: one bull, one ram, seven yearling lambs — a notably smaller sacrifice than the monthly new moon (two bulls). The reduction from the monthly new moon offering may reflect the new month's offering (v6: still required) being its own offering, with the holy assembly adding only the single-bull-ram-seven-lambs complement. The structural parallel to Yom Kippur (v8: same quantities) connects the New Year and the Day of Atonement as a unit — ten days of awe bracketed by matched offerings.
29.3: The grain offerings at the standard graduated scale: three-tenths per bull, two-tenths for the ram. The consistency of the grain-offering quantities across all major holy assemblies (Passover, Shavuot, Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur) establishes these four as structurally equivalent in the sacrificial system, even though their ceremonies differ significantly.
29.4: One-tenth per lamb for the seven Rosh Hashanah lambs. The grain offering total for a single holy assembly day: one bull (three-tenths) + one ram (two-tenths) + seven lambs (seven-tenths) = 1.2 ephahs of fine flour, plus approximately two hins of oil and substantial wine. The material scale of major festival observance represented significant community investment, reinforcing that covenant worship was not abstract but economically substantial.
29.5: One male goat as sin offering for Rosh Hashanah. The sin offering at the New Year frames the beginning of the new annual cycle with explicit acknowledgment of communal failure: the year begins not with presumption of righteousness but with atonement. This theological structure — atonement before celebration — characterizes Israel's entire approach to covenant renewal; the year's proper beginning is forgiveness rather than accomplishment.
29.6: All Rosh Hashanah offerings are in addition to the monthly new moon offering and the regular daily tamid. On Rosh Hashanah, then, the total offering is: tamid (2 lambs) + new moon (2 bulls, 1 ram, 7 lambs, 1 goat) + holy assembly (1 bull, 1 ram, 7 lambs, 1 goat). The day that begins the new year receives the maximum cumulative burden — an expression of intensified covenant acknowledgment at the annual threshold.
29.7: The tenth day of the seventh month is Yom Kippur — wəʿinnîtem ʾet-nap̄šōtêkem — the day of self-affliction. The offering law here specifies only the additional communal sacrifices; the central atonement ceremony of Yom Kippur (the two goats, the high priest's entry into the holy of holies, the scapegoat) is legislated in Lev 16. Num 29 provides the supplementary burnt offerings that accompany the Lev 16 ceremony.
29.8: The Yom Kippur offering: one bull, one ram, seven yearling lambs — identical to Rosh Hashanah (v2). The structural match between the New Year and the Day of Atonement may reflect the ancient link between the beginning of the year and its most solemn covenant renewal. The Ten Days of Awe between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur in later tradition formalize this connection: the year begins with the call to repentance (trumpets) and culminates in its enactment (fasting, confession, atonement).
29.9: The grain offerings at Yom Kippur follow the same scale as all major holy assemblies. The liturgical uniformity of Yom Kippur's supplementary offerings with those of other major festivals asserts that the Day of Atonement is a festival like the others in its sacrificial structure, even though its ceremony (Lev 16) is uniquely solemn. Yom Kippur is not liturgically isolated; it participates in the same covenant-meal framework as all other appointed times.
29.10: One-tenth per lamb for the seven lambs of Yom Kippur — the same as all other holy assemblies. The uniform one-tenth-per-lamb formula creates a recognizable liturgical signature for major festival days, distinguishing them from ordinary new moons and from the extraordinary Sukkot bull-series that follows.
29.11: One male goat as sin offering for Yom Kippur — in addition to (milelāḏ) the Yom Kippur sin offering legislated elsewhere (Lev 16's two goats). This verse explicitly acknowledges that Num 29's goat does not replace Lev 16's elaborate goat ceremony but supplements it. Yom Kippur thus has three sin-offering components: the Lev 16 ceremony (scapegoat + offered goat for the people) plus the Num 29 sin-offering goat, making it the most richly sin-atoning day in the calendar.
29.12: The fifteenth day of the seventh month — Sukkot (Tabernacles) — begins with a miqrāʾ-qōḏeš and a seven-day festival with no regular work. Wəḥaggōtem ḥag laYHWH šiḇʿat yāmîm — you shall celebrate a festival to YHWH for seven days. Sukkot is the most elaborate festival in the sacrificial calendar, featuring the largest and most unusual offering series in all of Numbers: the decreasing-bull sacrifice from 13 to 7 over seven days plus an eighth-day closing assembly.
29.13: Day 1 of Sukkot: thirteen young bulls — the highest number of bulls in the entire calendar. The bull-count descends by one each day: 13, 12, 11, 10, 9, 8, 7 — a total of 70 bulls over the seven days. The Talmud (Sukkah 55b) interprets the 70 bulls as representing the 70 nations of the world (from Gen 10's table of nations), making Sukkot an act of global intercession: Israel sacrifices on behalf of all humanity during this festival. This interpretation gives Sukkot a universal, not merely national, theological significance.
29.14: The grain offering for Day 1 of Sukkot: three-tenths per bull (13 × three-tenths = 39/10 ephahs for the bulls alone) plus two-tenths per ram. The total grain offering for Sukkot's first day far exceeds any other festival day, reflecting the festival's extraordinary sacrificial scale. The precision of specifying three-tenths per each of the thirteen bulls — requiring a separate calculation for each animal — reflects the P tradition's mathematical thoroughness.
29.15: One-tenth per lamb for the fourteen lambs on Day 1 of Sukkot. Two rams plus fourteen lambs appear consistently across all seven days of Sukkot; only the bull count changes. The consistent ram-and-lamb complement against the decreasing bulls creates a mathematical pattern that was recognized and interpreted by later Jewish tradition as deliberately structured rather than arbitrary.
29.16: One male goat as sin offering on Day 1 of Sukkot — the same sin-offering goat present at every major festival occasion. The daily sin-offering goat throughout Sukkot's seven days means that each day of the festival includes both its own burnt-offering celebration and its own atonement component. The seven successive sin offerings ensure that the extended celebration of Sukkot does not accumulate unaddressed sin.
29.17: Day 2 of Sukkot: twelve bulls. The single-bull decrease from 13 to 12 begins the descending pattern that structures the entire festival's sacrificial logic. The symmetry of the descent — consistent single-unit reduction per day — creates a countdown toward the festival's end, each day slightly less elaborate than the previous. The descent from abundance to sufficiency mirrors the movement from harvest's plenty to covenant sufficiency.
29.18: Their grain and drink offerings for Day 2 are according to their number — the standard cross-reference formula used throughout Days 2-7 rather than restating every quantity. The formula kannišpāṭ — according to the ordinance/rule — establishes that the graduated grain and drink accompaniments of Num 15 govern all these offerings, creating a compact legal cross-reference system.
29.19: One goat as sin offering on Day 2 of Sukkot — the pattern holds daily. The repetition of the sin offering across all seven days of Sukkot has led scholars to read it as a daily renewal of atonement — not merely a single atoning act for the festival period but a daily covenant reset during the most elaborate festival of the year.
29.20: Day 3: eleven bulls. The decreasing series continues its mathematically regular pattern. The offering specifications for Days 3-7 (vv20-34) follow identical structural templates: N bulls + 2 rams + 14 lambs + grain offerings per number + 1 goat, all in addition to the regular tamid. The structural regularity makes the decreasing bull count the single variable that distinguishes each day from the others.
29.21: Grain and drink offerings according to the ordinance for Day 3's bulls, rams, and lambs. The kannišpāṭ formula efficiently covers the full graduated offering complement without restating what Num 15:1-12 established as the standing rule. The legal cross-reference system presupposes that users of the calendar have ch15 available; Numbers is a cumulative legal document, not a standalone code.
29.22: One goat as sin offering on Day 3. The consistent presence of the daily goat sin offering throughout Sukkot maintains the dual character of the festival — celebration and atonement in tension. Israel's liturgical calendar refuses to separate joy from acknowledgment of failure; each day of the harvest celebration is also a day of covenant humility.
29.23: Day 4: ten bulls. The decreasing sequence continues through the middle days of the festival, each day's offering slightly diminished from the previous. By Day 4, the bull count has fallen to ten — exactly half the totals implied by the series' beginning and end. The mathematical pattern suggests intentional design: the festival moves from maximum sacrificial expression to minimum.
29.24: Grain and drink offerings per the standing ordinance for Day 4. The compact cross-reference formula is used for all middle days of Sukkot; the legislative efficiency reflects the priestly compiler's assumption that readers understand the system and need only the variable (bull count) specified.
29.25: One goat as sin offering on Day 4. The daily goat ensures that the festival's middle days, despite their less elaborate offerings, maintain the atonement dimension. No day of the seven passes without the offering that addresses sin — a structural assertion that sin does not take a holiday during Israel's most celebrated festival.
29.26: Day 5: nine bulls. The descent continues past the midpoint. Days 5-7 form the closing arc of the festival's offering series, moving from nine to seven. The final number seven — matching the number of days in the festival — creates a numeric culmination: the last day's bull count equals the festival's total length, a closure by coincidence or design.
