The entire legal edifice of the chapter — cities of refuge, two witnesses, no ransom, blood atoning for blood — rests on this foundation: YHWH's presence in the land. Where God dwells, moral order must be maintained. The legal system is not merely practical but theological: it keeps the land fit for the presence of the holy God who has chosen to inhabit it.

36.1: The family heads of the Manassite clan of Gilead approach Moses with a legal problem — a case study in how the earlier inheritance ruling for Zelophehad's daughters (ch27:1-11) creates an unintended consequence for tribal land continuity. The petition is respectful and legally precise: they do not contest the prior ruling but identify its systemic implication.
36.2: They cite YHWH's command to give the land by lot and YHWH's command to give Zelophehad's inheritance to his daughters. Both commands are YHWH's; the problem arises not from anyone's disobedience but from the collision of two legitimate principles — daughters' inheritance rights and tribal land continuity. The text presents this as a genuine legal tension, not a bad-faith challenge.
36.3: The problem: if the daughters marry men from other tribes, their inherited land follows them into the new tribe. At the jubilee, land returns to its tribe — but the daughters' land, now belonging to another tribe by their marriage, would transfer permanently. The jubilee's function (Lev 25) was to prevent permanent alienation of ancestral land, but marriage creates an exception the jubilee cannot correct.
36.4: The jubilee year (yovel) — every fifty years, all land reverts to its original tribal allocation, and all debt-slaves are freed (Lev 25). The Zelophehad daughters' case exposes a gap in the jubilee mechanism: it can reverse commercial land sales but not land transferred by inheritance through inter-tribal marriage. The clan leaders identify a genuine loophole in the land-protection system.
36.5: YHWH vindicates the petition: 'The tribe of the sons of Joseph is right.' The recognition of the complaint's legitimacy is significant — not every legal petition receives divine validation. This one does. The prior ruling favoring daughters' inheritance was also right; both rulings are right, and the resolution must honor both.
36.6: The resolution: the daughters may marry anyone they choose, but within their father's tribe's clan. Ba-tov be'eineihem tihyenah lenashim — 'let them be wives to whom it seems good in their eyes.' The freedom of choice is preserved — no specific husbands are appointed. The only restriction is tribal endogamy. The narrowest possible constraint that resolves the land-continuity problem without coercing the women into specific marriages.
36.7: 'So that no inheritance of the Israelites shall transfer from tribe to tribe.' The principle stated here becomes permanent law (v8). The land distribution system's integrity requires that tribal boundaries be maintained not just by administrative fiat but by the marriage choices of heiresses. The social and legal systems reinforce each other: the jubilee handles commercial transfers; the marriage rule handles inheritance transfers.
36.8: The ruling generalizes beyond Zelophehad's daughters: every daughter who possesses an inheritance in any Israelite tribe must marry within her father's tribal clan. The case law becomes precedent. This is standard ancient Near Eastern legal reasoning: a case resolution establishes a rule that applies to all similar cases. The specific case of five named women becomes universal law.
36.9: 'No inheritance shall transfer from one tribe to another' — the principle is absolute and permanent. The Nuzi tablets (15th-14th century BCE) from Mesopotamia contain similar provisions for daughters who inherit: they were often required to marry within the family to preserve the estate. Israel's provision is wider (the tribe, not just the immediate family) but participates in the same logic: inheritance preservation through controlled marriage.
36.10: The daughters of Zelophehad did as YHWH commanded Moses. Five women comply with the new ruling. Their names are recalled — Mahlah, Tirzah, Hoglah, Milcah, and Noah — the same five who petitioned Moses in ch27. The narrative circle closes: the women who sought an inheritance now preserve it through their obedience. Some of these names appear as place names in the Samaria Ostraca (8th century BCE), suggesting the daughters' names were later given to towns in the Manasseh region.
36.11: They married within the sons of their father's brothers — cousins within the clan of Manasseh. The text specifies 'father's brothers' children' — first-cousin marriage, which was standard and even preferred in many ancient Near Eastern cultures for exactly the land-preservation reason given here. Endogamy kept property and allegiances within defined kinship networks.
36.12: Their inheritance remained in the tribe of their father's clan. The outcome vindicates the ruling and the women's obedience: the land of Zelophehad stays with Manasseh. The jubilee mechanism works as designed; the tribal allocation is intact. Five women's choices preserve a tribal inheritance across generations.
36.13: 'These are the commandments and the statutes which YHWH commanded by the hand of Moses to the Israelites in the plains of Moab by the Jordan opposite Jericho.' The closing formula gathers all of Numbers 28-36 under a single inscription and anchors the entire book at a specific moment: the plains of Moab, facing Jericho, on the threshold of the land. The book of Numbers ends exactly where Deuteronomy will begin — not with an arrival but with a readiness, the people at the threshold, the Torah given, the promise one step away.
